steps.text: Collect (sample) the actual combustible dusts at the facility.
Test the collected dusts at OSHA’s Salt Lake Technical Center or at another licensed laboratory using recognized methods.
Evaluate the specific conditions of the hazard and the objectives of protection to choose the most effective and reliable means for explosion control.
Provide deflagration venting to a safe area in accordance with NFPA 68.
Protect the equipment from the effects of a deflagration by deflagration venting, deflagration suppression, or deflagration pressure containment.

Develop and implement a written housekeeping program that establishes the frequency and method(s) determined best to reduce accumulations of fugitive grain dust on ledges, floors, equipment, and other exposed surfaces.
Immediately remove any fugitive grain dust accumulations at priority housekeeping areas, or demonstrate and assure that equivalent protection is provided.
Review the applicable NFPA standard(s) to ensure that the criteria for the dust hazards are understood.
Contact your supervisor, the SLTC laboratory, or the HRT for assistance.
Perform an initial evaluation of the space and the dust accumulations.
Take thickness measurements of dust accumulations.
Measure the length, width, and height of rooms or building enclosures where combustible dusts are present.
Measure the length, width, and depth of dust accumulations in each area.
Measure the size of each dust accumulation area if dust is not present throughout the room or building enclosure.
Take strategically documented photographs of overhead accumulations if measurements of elevated surfaces are not feasible.
Select locations and collect a representative sample.
Ask managers and engineers for a copy of the bill of materials to verify if combustible dust handling equipment and systems are constructed of non-combustible materials.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the written procedures and safe work practices to verify if facilities, equipment, and systems are operated to prevent fires, deflagrations, and explosions.

Verify if the system is being properly maintained (inspected and tested) by asking questions, reviewing AMS design and field installation, identifying markings, obtaining/reviewing installation documentation, reviewing applicable NFPA standards, and obtaining/reviewing manufacturer’s information.

Determine if the AMS contains any recognized methods of active or passive isolation to prevent deflagration propagation out of all AMS openings (such as the dirty air inlet, clean air exhaust outlet, and material discharge hopper).
If isolation devices are present, obtain details (type, manufacturer, model, and year of installation) and verify if each system is being properly maintained (inspected and tested).
Verify if the ducting terminates at a safe, outdoor location away from employees and any air inlets.

Ask questions, review AMS design and field installation, identify markings, obtain/review installation documentation, review applicable NFPA standards, and obtain/review manufacturer’s information regarding isolation devices.
Verify if the AMS has recognized methods of detecting a fire condition and mitigating the hazard.
Obtain details (type, manufacturer, model, and year of installation) and verify if the system is being maintained (inspected and tested) per the manufacturer’s recommendations.
Determine if there are any upstream controls in place to detect a fire condition and provide a response, and if so, describe the system.
Contact supervisor for direction if suspecting dusts to be sampled may have other hazards.

Obtain approval of your area office, in consultation with other offices and teams as necessary, before collecting samples of combustible dust hybrid mixtures or dusts with other hazardous characteristics.
Apply the sample seal.
Remove the heel grounders and bonding wires.
Notify the employer that sampling at that location has been completed.
Weigh crucible.
Weigh aliquot of sample in crucible.
Send the sample bottle, along with the depth and area measurements, to the lab.
Dry the sample for 24 hours in a drying oven set at 167°F or 75°C.
Weigh the sample.
Design the actuated pinch valve system using the manufacturer's recommendations.
Install the actuated pinch valves using the manufacturer's recommendations.
Maintain the actuated pinch valves using the manufacturer's recommendations.
See NFPA 77 for detailed guidance.

preconditions.text: Need to specifically determine the characteristics of the actual combustible dusts at a given facility.
A building or building compartment contains a dust explosion hazard outside of equipment.
An explosion hazard exists within any operating equipment greater than 8 ft3 (0.2 m3) of containing volume.
Fugitive grain dust accumulations exceed 1/8 inch (0.32 cm) at priority housekeeping areas.
There is a question about the provisions or use of these standards.
If dust accumulations are obviously less than 5% of the floor area, measurements may not be needed.
Measurements have been taken (and other appropriate documentation made).
There is no isolation device on the clean air exhaust outlet
The AMS has recognized methods of detecting a fire condition and mitigating the hazard
Ensure the dust is not a low-MIE dust (otherwise follow Appendix H).
Ensure the dust is not a metal dust (otherwise follow Appendix I).
Dusts with more than 5% moisture are dried prior to performing explosibility tests.
The sample is received at the SLTC lab.

constraints.text: Do not rely solely on published combustible dust data to adequately or accurately represent actual dust characteristics or hazards at a given location.
Rely on the explosion parameters of the dusts in your own processes (e.g., ASTM test methods or equivalent) when designing or selecting engineering controls for explosion protection.
Do not follow this OTM chapter or the NEP for explosives and blasting agents.
Do not collect samples of explosives or blasting agents.
29 CFR 1910.132, Personal Protective Equipment General Requirements, applies to personal protective equipment (PPE) for dust hazards.
Venting of deflagrations only reduces the damage that results from combustion.
If an OSHA standard specifically refers to or incorporates an older version of an industry standard, the older version applies.

CSHOs should send samples to SLTC for bulk density determination only if the material is light (such as paper dust or fabric fibers) and the levels of accumulations are greater than ¼ inch extending over 5% of the floor area of a room or a building, or 1000 ft2, whichever is less.
Measurements should not include spillage from openings in equipment where the spillage does not meet the definition of a fugitive combustible dust.
CSHO safety during sampling is paramount.
Samples should be collected as early as possible.
Follow proper sampling procedures delineated in Appendix G, Appendix H, and Appendix I.

High-speed abort gates are not deflagration propagation isolation devices. They are for temporarily aborting air to a safe location during a fire condition to protect employees from the hazardous byproducts of a fire. They do not react quickly enough in normal installations to adequately prevent propagating flame front transmission, and they are not recognized by NFPA as deflagration propagation isolation devices.
Wear PPE described in the NEP.
This test method must be modified when the materials being tested degrade at 75ºC.
Appropriate selection based on properties of the material is key
Maintaining optimal conditions (e.g., temperatures, or feed rates)
bonding/grounding
fire detection/suppression
The design of the system should be based on the worst case scenario properties of the particulate representative within the vessel at the facility’s process.
Relies heavily on the use of conductive components.
Personnel bonding and grounding is generally not necessary when flammable gases, vapors, and hybrid mixtures are not present and where the MIE of the dust cloud is greater than 30 mJ.
Intrinsic Safety: A spark or thermal effect is incapable of causing ignition of a flammable or combustible material in air under prescribed test methods.
NEMA Type 9 enclosures.
